findings.claim: The evaluation design (site-stratified temporal split) adequately controls for distribution shift and ensures that the Transformer's performance on the held-out cross-site test set reflects true generalization to unseen hospital systems.
findings.failure_mechanism: The design assumes that temporal stratification and site stratification are sufficient to isolate cross-site generalization effects. However, hospital systems may differ in ways beyond just site identity: reporting templates, scanner types, radiologist cohorts, and clinical protocols may evolve over time independently at each site. The held-out cross-site test set is from the most recent 12 months, which may coincide with a period of rapid template standardization or EHR changes across systems. Thus, the cross-site test may not reflect true site-specific generalization, but rather a conflation of site and temporal effects. The design does not include a 'site-by-time' interaction analysis to disentangle these effects.
findings.evidence_test: Run a two-way ANOVA or interaction analysis on cross-site test performance, with factors: (a) site identity and (b) time period (e.g., pre/post-2020). If the site-by-time interaction is significant, the cross-site result is confounded and cannot be interpreted as pure site generalization.

findings.claim: Report-level recall is an appropriate primary metric for clinical screening of follow-up recommendations.
findings.failure_mechanism: Report-level recall measures whether the model flags *any* follow-up span in a report that contains at least one follow-up recommendation. This metric is insensitive to false positives and to the number of missed spans within a report. A model could achieve high recall by detecting a single, easy-to-find follow-up span while missing all others, especially in long reports with multiple recommendations. This metric does not penalize precision errors at the report level, which could lead to alert fatigue in a real clinical screening system. The metric also does not distinguish between detecting a single recommendation vs. detecting all recommendations — a critical distinction for registry completeness.
findings.evidence_test: Compute per-report F1 (harmonic mean of per-report precision and recall) and compare to report-level recall. If per-report F1 is substantially lower than report-level recall, the metric is masking precision failures. Also, compute 'completeness' — the fraction of reports where *all* follow-up spans are detected — and compare across models.

findings.claim: The Transformer and CRF models are trained with comparable computational effort and hyperparameter tuning budgets, enabling a fair comparison.
findings.failure_mechanism: While both models undergo hyperparameter tuning and early stopping, the Transformer is a large pretrained model with millions of parameters, while the CRF is a linear model with thousands of features. The tuning budget (e.g., number of hyperparameter combinations tried) may not be sufficient to find the CRF's optimal configuration due to its larger feature space and sensitivity to feature scaling. The CRF's performance may be under-optimized relative to the Transformer's, biasing the comparison in favor of the Transformer.
findings.evidence_test: Run a resource-balanced tuning experiment: allocate the same total compute time (e.g., GPU hours for Transformer, CPU hours for CRF) to each model's hyperparameter search. If CRF's performance improves significantly with more tuning, the original comparison is biased.

findings.claim: The held-out cross-site test set is representative of the deployment distribution for follow-up recommendation extraction.
findings.failure_mechanism: The cross-site test set is drawn from a single hospital system's most recent 12 months. If this system has atypical reporting practices (e.g., a specialized oncology center with unique follow-up protocols), the results may not generalize to other unseen hospital systems. The design does not include a 'diversity check' to ensure the held-out system is representative of the broader distribution of hospital systems the model is intended to serve.
findings.evidence_test: Compute a representativeness score for the held-out system (e.g., using a proxy model trained on all systems except the held-out one, and measuring its performance on the held-out system). If performance is near the lower end of the in-distribution range, the system is atypical. Alternatively, cluster hospital systems by report characteristics (e.g., TF-IDF centroids) and ensure the held-out system is not an outlier.

findings.claim: The CRF baseline with TF-IDF + hand-engineered features is a strong and appropriate baseline for follow-up recommendation extraction.
findings.failure_mechanism: The CRF baseline relies on a manually curated keyword list for follow-up detection. This list may be incomplete or biased toward the training systems' reporting styles, especially for rare or domain-specific follow-up phrases. The hand-engineered features (e.g., token length, case information) may not capture the linguistic patterns in follow-up recommendations as effectively as learned representations. The baseline may be artificially weak, inflating the Transformer's apparent advantage.
findings.evidence_test: Run an ablation where the CRF uses a larger, automatically extracted keyword set (e.g., from training labels) or a learned representation (e.g., averaged word embeddings). If performance improves significantly, the baseline is weak.

findings.claim: The annotation guidelines and inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa ≥0.75) ensure high-quality labels for training and evaluation.
findings.failure_mechanism: Cohen's kappa is sensitive to class imbalance. If follow-up recommendations are rare (e.g., <5% of tokens), kappa can be artificially inflated even with high disagreement on the minority class. The guidelines may not adequately address edge cases like negated or conditional follow-ups, leading to systematic label noise that affects both models similarly but may bias the evaluation toward models that mimic the labelers' biases.
findings.evidence_test: Compute per-class kappa and agreement rates for follow-up vs. non-follow-up spans. If kappa for follow-up spans is <0.7, the label quality for the minority class is questionable. Also, audit a sample of disagreements to check for systematic biases (e.g., negation handling).

findings.claim: The evaluation design controls for template leakage by measuring character 4-gram similarity.
findings.failure_mechanism: Character 4-gram similarity is a coarse measure of template similarity and may miss subtle but critical differences in follow-up phrasing or structure. For example, two reports could have identical templates but differ in the follow-up clause, or vice versa. The threshold (>0.8 similarity) is arbitrary and not tied to a measurable impact on model performance.
findings.evidence_test: Replace 4-gram similarity with a task-specific similarity metric (e.g., cosine similarity of TF-IDF vectors restricted to follow-up-related terms). If the metric correlates better with model performance differences, it is more appropriate.

critic_r1_output.summary: The PoC has two FATAL flaws: (1) the cross-site evaluation conflates site and temporal effects, and (2) the primary metric (report-level recall) masks precision failures and does not measure clinical screening utility. These invalidate the central claims about generalization and screening performance. MATERIAL flaws include potential baseline bias and weak representativeness of the held-out system.

defender_r1_output.pass_1_analysis:
F1 (FATAL 8, distribution_shift): The critic claims the site-stratified temporal split conflates site and temporal effects because the held-out cross-site test (one system's most recent 12 months) may capture global changes like template standardization, without a site-by-time interaction analysis. The methodology explicitly designs the split to test two generalization axes: in-distribution (same sites, future time via 20% recent in-dist test) and cross-site (entirely unseen hospital system, recent 12 months). Section 2 justifies this as 'forcing the model to generalize beyond the training distribution' and separates temporal (within sites) from site-shift. The held-out is deliberately unseen site + recent time to mimic real deployment (new hospital with current practices), not to isolate pure site effects holding time constant. Confounds section includes template leakage check (4-gram similarity), but no interaction analysis because the hypothesis (section 7: 'generalizes beyond the training hospital systems') encompasses compound shifts. Evidence is uncertain without running ANOVA, but design intent resolves via scope/choice—no leakage by construction (unseen site), and temporal is controlled separately via in-dist test. Fully rebuttable as deliberate design.

F2 (FATAL 7, metric_limitations): Critic argues report-level recall ignores FPs, multiple misses per report, and precision, potentially masking issues for screening/registry. Methodology section 5 explicitly defines it as primary for 'clinical screening goal of not missing reports that contain actionable follow-up language... reduces sensitivity to small span-boundary differences', with secondaries (per-entity F1, span-level P/R/F1, per-site F1, completeness implied via span metrics) to 'characterize fidelity'. This directly addresses insensitivity: recall prioritizes not missing reports, secondaries catch precision/multi-span issues. Scope (section 7) limits to screening recall superiority, not full extraction fidelity. No uncertainty—design choice eliminates the failure mechanism by aligning metric to goal and supplementing.

F3 (MATERIAL 6, missing_baselines): Claims tuning budgets comparable, but critic says CRF's larger feature space needs more tuning than Transformer's pretrained capacity. Section 4 specifies identical grids (LR/batch for Transformer, L2 for CRF), early stopping, 'same hyperparameter tuning budget and effort as Transformer', 'identical computational resources and development time'. Confounds include capacity parity (param/feature counts) and representation ablation (Transformer with CRF features). This controls for optimization bias—fair effort despite architecture differences, standard for baseline comparisons. No evidence CRF under-optimized; design justifies parity.

F4 (MATERIAL 5, distribution_shift): Held-out cross-site representative of deployment. Critic: single system may be atypical (e.g., oncology center). Methodology sources from '3–4 distinct hospital systems', holds out one (~5-8k reports), tests generalization to 'a different hospital system' (section 7). Scope explicitly: 'from the same sites and from a different hospital system'—not broader representativeness, but binary (train sites vs. one unseen). In-dist test covers site effects within train systems; no claim of 'deployment distribution' beyond this PoC. Eliminates concern via scope.

F5 (MATERIAL 4, missing_baselines): CRF baseline strong. Critic: manual keywords incomplete/biased. Section 3 details TF-IDF (5k vocab unigrams/bigrams, fit on train), hand-features (curated keywords like 'repeat, follow-up', position, length, case)—standard for NER baselines. Comparable to Transformer features (tokens/masks). Confounds: representation ablation isolates learned reps. Keywords curated domain-specifically; no auto-extraction needed for fair baseline (non-deep). Design choice resolves.

F6 (MINOR 3, implicit_dist_assump): Kappa >=0.75 ensures quality, but critic: imbalance inflates, edge cases like negation. Section 1: panel annotation, adjudication, guidelines version-locked, document negation/conditional explicitly in label def. Confounds: dual-annotate 5% per split, report IRA separately (enables per-class), class imbalance flagged. Directly anticipates; per-class kappa feasible post-hoc. Resolved.

F7 (MINOR 2, silent_misconfig): 4-gram similarity controls leakage. Critic: coarse, arbitrary threshold. Confounds section: specific check (>5% test reports >0.8 similar to train)—sanity flag, not sole control. Unseen site + temporal split prevents leakage by construction (no overlap). Threshold documented; impact on metric low for PoC. Minor quibble.

rebuttals.justification: The split deliberately tests compound site + temporal generalization to an unseen recent hospital (section 2: 'cross-site (different hospital, potentially different... templates)'), with in-dist test isolating temporal within sites; no need for interaction analysis as hypothesis (section 7) targets beyond-training-systems performance, not pure site isolation.

Report-level recall is explicitly chosen for screening 'not missing reports' (section 5), with secondaries (span-level F1, per-entity F1) addressing precision/multi-span fidelity; design eliminates insensitivity by goal alignment and supplementation.

Identical tuning grids, budgets, effort, resources (section 4), plus capacity parity and representation ablation (confounds) ensure fair optimization despite architecture; standard for comparing pretrained deep vs. classical baselines.

Scope (section 7) tests generalization to 'a different hospital system' from 3-4 distinct ones, not representativeness to broader deployment; single held-out directly matches experiment claim of cross-site generalization.

Curated domain keywords + TF-IDF/position features (section 3) are standard strong NER baseline; representation ablation isolates Transformer advantage, ensuring fair comparison without auto-features.
Guidelines address negation/conditional explicitly (section 1); dual-annotation per split + IRA reporting (confounds) enables per-class checks, resolving imbalance/edge-case concerns by design.
4-gram check is a documented sanity flag (confounds) atop no-overlap split; arbitrary threshold has negligible impact on unseen-site generalization metric validity for PoC.

defender_r1_output.verdict_rationale: All findings rebutted with design choices, scope limits, or immateriality, yielding adjusted severities ≤3; methodology sound as-intended with explicit controls matching claims. No concessions or deferrals needed.

challenges.reasoning: The rebuttal argues that the split tests compound site+temporal generalization and that interaction analysis is unnecessary because the hypothesis targets beyond-training-systems performance. However, this does not address the core failure mechanism: the conflation of site and temporal effects in the cross-site test set. The defender does not provide evidence that the held-out cross-site test set is temporally aligned with the training data or that temporal effects are controlled. The claim that 'no need for interaction analysis' is a scope disclaimer, not a mechanistic rebuttal of the confounding. The failure mechanism (site-by-time interaction) remains unresolved.

The rebuttal correctly identifies that report-level recall is chosen for screening ('not missing reports') and that secondary metrics (span-level F1, per-entity F1) address precision and multi-span fidelity. This directly addresses the failure mechanism by aligning the metric with the clinical goal and supplementing it with more granular metrics. The argument is specific and mechanistic.

The rebuttal cites identical tuning grids, budgets, resources, and additional controls (capacity parity, representation ablation) to ensure fair optimization. This directly addresses the concern that the CRF's performance may be under-optimized relative to the Transformer. The argument is specific and mechanistic.

The rebuttal correctly identifies that the scope of the experiment is to test generalization to 'a different hospital system' rather than representativeness to a broader deployment. This is a valid scope disclaimer that aligns with the experiment's claims. The finding is outside the scope of the experiment's goals.

The rebuttal cites the use of curated domain keywords + TF-IDF/position features as a standard strong NER baseline and notes that the representation ablation isolates the Transformer's advantage. This directly addresses the concern that the baseline may be artificially weak. The argument is specific and mechanistic.

The rebuttal cites explicit addressing of negation/conditional in guidelines and dual-annotation per split + IRA reporting as enabling per-class checks. This directly addresses the failure mechanism by ensuring label quality for minority classes and edge cases. The argument is specific and mechanistic.

The rebuttal correctly identifies that the 4-gram check is a documented sanity flag with negligible impact on the unseen-site generalization metric validity for a PoC. This is a valid scope disclaimer that the metric is immaterial to the experiment's goals.

defender_r2_output.pass_2_analysis: Contesting the single CHALLENGE on F1 (conflation of site/temporal effects) by strengthening the mechanistic rebuttal: both test sets are temporally aligned as 'recent' holds against older training data, parallelizing temporal shift to isolate site effects via relative performance comparison—no further interaction analysis needed. Maintaining all ACCEPT positions unchanged as they directly resolve the original concerns.

rebuttals.justification: The design mechanistically controls conflation via parallel temporal stratification: both in-distribution test ('most recent 20%') and cross-site test ('most recent 12 months') are recent-time holds against older training data from source systems (oldest 60%), aligning temporal shifts symmetrically across tests. Relative drops (cross-site vs. in-dist) thus isolate site effects beyond shared temporal evolution (e.g., global template changes affect both equally); per-site reporting on in-dist further enables site-time diagnostics. This directly reflects realistic 'beyond-training-systems' generalization (section 2/7), eliminating the claimed confound without needing post-hoc ANOVA.

Report-level recall is explicitly chosen for screening 'not missing reports' (section 5), with secondaries (span-level F1, per-entity F1) addressing precision/multi-span fidelity; design eliminates insensitivity by goal alignment and supplementation.

Identical tuning grids, budgets, effort, resources (section 4), plus capacity parity and representation ablation (confounds) ensure fair optimization despite architecture; standard for comparing pretrained deep vs. classical baselines.

Scope (section 7) tests generalization to 'a different hospital system' from 3-4 distinct ones, not representativeness to broader deployment; single held-out directly matches experiment claim of cross-site generalization.

Curated domain keywords + TF-IDF/position features (section 3) are standard strong NER baseline; representation ablation isolates Transformer advantage, ensuring fair comparison without auto-features.
Guidelines address negation/conditional explicitly (section 1); dual-annotation per split + IRA reporting (confounds) enables per-class checks, resolving imbalance/edge-case concerns by design.
4-gram check is a documented sanity flag (confounds) atop no-overlap split; arbitrary threshold has negligible impact on unseen-site generalization metric validity for PoC.

defender_r2_output.verdict_rationale: All findings, including the challenged F1, are rebutted to adjusted severity ≤3 with no CONCEDE or DEFER outcomes. The strengthened mechanistic control for F1 confirms the design fully addresses distribution shift concerns within scope.
